title: Anaphylaxis
objectives: Recognize the clinical criteria for diagnosing anaphylaxis
Understand the immunologic mechanisms of IgE-mediated reactions
Apply appropriate first-line treatment with epinephrine
Identify common triggers and cross-reactivity patterns
Manage refractory anaphylaxis and biphasic reactions
Counsel patients on prevention and emergency preparedness

vignette.text:
A 34-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after collapsing at a restaurant. She had just started eating a Thai shrimp dish when she developed sudden throat tightness and difficulty breathing. She felt lightheaded and collapsed. EMS found her hypotensive and administered intramuscular epinephrine en route.

Past Medical History: Allergic rhinitis, Asthma (mild intermittent), Known allergy to shellfish (previous reaction: hives after eating crab 2 years ago)

Medications: Fluticasone nasal spray PRN, Albuterol inhaler PRN, Cetirizine 10mg daily during allergy season

Social History: Marketing manager, no smoking, social alcohol, exercises regularly

Allergies: Shellfish (hives), Sulfa drugs (rash)

physicalExam.general: Anxious woman, voice is hoarse, audible stridor, sitting upright
physicalExam.HEENT: Angioedema of lips and tongue, uvula edematous, oropharynx erythematous
physicalExam.pulmonary: Diffuse expiratory wheezes, decreased air movement, stridor on inspiration
physicalExam.skin: Diffuse urticaria (raised, erythematous wheals) over trunk and extremities, warm and flushed
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, mild diffuse tenderness, hyperactive bowel sounds
physicalExam.extremities: No edema, warm with weak pulses

Tryptase.value: Sent (draw within 1-2 hours of symptom onset)

other.ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 128 bpm, no ST changes

questions.stem: She has angioedema of lips and tongue with stridor.
questions.question: Angioedema affecting which anatomical structure is most life-threatening?
questions.hint: Consider airway anatomy.

options.text: Larynx and epiglottis - can cause complete airway obstruction

questions.explanation: Laryngeal angioedema is the most dangerous form - swelling of the epiglottis, arytenoids, and vocal cords can cause complete upper airway obstruction. Stridor indicates significant narrowing. Lip and tongue swelling can progress to involve the larynx. Early intubation may be needed.

questions.stem: Mast cells are central to the allergic response.
questions.question: Where are mast cells predominantly located in the body?
questions.hint: They are positioned to encounter allergens at body surfaces.

options.text: Skin, respiratory mucosa, GI mucosa, and around blood vessels

questions.explanation: Mast cells are tissue-resident cells strategically located at host-environment interfaces: skin, respiratory tract, GI tract, and perivascular spaces. This positioning allows rapid response to allergens at entry points, explaining the pattern of anaphylaxis symptoms.

questions.stem: Epinephrine is given intramuscularly.
questions.question: What is the recommended injection site for IM epinephrine in anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Optimal absorption requires good blood flow to muscle.
questions.explanation: The anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) provides fastest absorption due to excellent blood supply. Studies show peak plasma levels reached faster than deltoid or subcutaneous routes. Auto-injectors are designed for thigh injection through clothing if needed.

questions.stem: She has hypotension (82/50) and tachycardia.
questions.question: What causes the cardiovascular collapse in anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Consider the effects of histamine and other mediators on vessels.

options.text: Massive vasodilation and increased vascular permeability causing distributive shock and third-spacing

questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis causes distributive shock: histamine, prostaglandins, and leukotrienes cause profound vasodilation (decreased SVR) and increased capillary permeability (plasma extravasation). Up to 35% of intravascular volume can shift to extravascular space within minutes.

whyChain.answer: Tachycardia is a compensatory response to hypotension (baroreceptor reflex) and direct effect of epinephrine/catecholamines released during stress. Also, histamine directly increases heart rate via H2 receptors.

questions.stem: She has diffuse wheezing and stridor.
questions.question: What causes the respiratory manifestations of anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Multiple mechanisms affect the airways.

options.text: Upper airway: angioedema causing obstruction; Lower airway: bronchospasm from mediators

questions.explanation: Respiratory symptoms have two components: (1) Upper airway - angioedema of larynx/pharynx causes stridor/obstruction; (2) Lower airway - histamine and leukotrienes cause bronchospasm (wheezing), mucus hypersecretion. Her asthma history increases bronchospasm risk.

questions.stem: She has urticaria (hives) covering her trunk and extremities.
questions.question: What causes urticaria in anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Consider histamine's effects on skin vasculature.

options.text: Histamine causes local vasodilation and increased permeability in dermal vessels

questions.explanation: Urticaria results from mast cell degranulation in skin releasing histamine. Histamine causes: (1) Vasodilation → erythema/flare; (2) Increased permeability → localized edema (wheal); (3) Sensory nerve stimulation → pruritus. The classic 'wheal and flare' response.

questions.stem: Tryptase level was sent.
questions.question: What does serum tryptase measure and when should it be drawn?
questions.hint: Tryptase is released from mast cells.

options.text: Mast cell degranulation marker; draw within 15 min-3 hours of symptom onset (peaks 1-2 hours)

questions.explanation: Tryptase is a protease released from mast cells during degranulation. It peaks 1-2 hours after anaphylaxis onset and remains elevated for several hours. Elevated tryptase supports anaphylaxis diagnosis. Normal tryptase doesn't rule out anaphylaxis (especially food-triggered).

questions.stem: She has a known shellfish allergy and reacted to shrimp.
questions.question: What is the immunologic mechanism of IgE-mediated anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: This is a Type I hypersensitivity reaction.

options.text: Allergen crosslinks IgE on mast cells → degranulation → release of histamine and other mediators

questions.explanation: Type I (IgE-mediated): Prior sensitization produces allergen-specific IgE that binds mast cell FcεRI receptors. Re-exposure → allergen crosslinks IgE → mast cell degranulation → immediate release of preformed mediators (histamine, tryptase) and synthesis of new mediators (prostaglandins, leukotrienes).

whyChain.why: Why was her previous reaction milder (just hives)?
whyChain.answer: Allergic reactions can be unpredictable. Each exposure may be more or less severe depending on allergen dose, route, and individual factors. Prior mild reaction doesn't predict future severity - this is why epinephrine is prescribed for any IgE-mediated food allergy.

questions.stem: She is allergic to shellfish (crab) and reacted to shrimp.
questions.question: What explains cross-reactivity between different shellfish?
questions.hint: Shellfish share common allergenic proteins.

options.text: Tropomyosin is the major allergen shared across crustaceans (shrimp, crab, lobster)
Cross-reactivity doesn't occur in food allergy

questions.explanation: Tropomyosin is a highly conserved muscle protein that is the major allergen in crustaceans. Cross-reactivity within crustaceans (shrimp, crab, lobster, crawfish) is ~75%. Mollusks (clam, oyster, scallop) share some cross-reactivity but less than crustaceans. Fish allergy is separate (parvalbumin allergen).

questions.stem: Her asthma may worsen anaphylaxis severity.
questions.question: Why is asthma a risk factor for severe/fatal anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Consider the lower airway response.

options.text: Pre-existing airway hyperreactivity amplifies bronchospasm during anaphylaxis
Asthma has no effect on anaphylaxis

questions.explanation: Asthma is a major risk factor for fatal anaphylaxis. The hyperreactive airways respond exaggeratedly to histamine and leukotrienes, causing severe bronchospasm. Poorly controlled asthma particularly increases risk. Food allergies + asthma is a high-risk combination.

questions.stem: Anaphylaxis can occur without IgE involvement.
questions.question: What are non-IgE-mediated mechanisms of anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Some reactions bypass the IgE pathway.

options.text: Direct mast cell activation (radiocontrast, opioids), complement activation, or IgG-mediated
Only bacterial toxins cause non-IgE anaphylaxis

questions.explanation: Non-IgE anaphylaxis (formerly 'anaphylactoid'): (1) Direct mast cell activation - radiocontrast, opioids, vancomycin; (2) Complement activation; (3) IgG-mediated (rare). Clinical presentation identical to IgE-mediated; treatment is the same. Now all called 'anaphylaxis' regardless of mechanism.

questions.stem: Epinephrine is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis.
questions.question: What is the correct dose and route of epinephrine for anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: IM is preferred over IV for initial treatment.

options.text: 0.3-0.5 mg (0.01 mg/kg in children) IM in anterolateral thigh; can repeat every 5-15 minutes

questions.explanation: Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg (1:1000 concentration = 1 mg/mL) IM in anterolateral thigh. Auto-injectors: 0.3 mg for adults, 0.15 mg for children <30 kg. Can repeat every 5-15 minutes if needed. IM preferred - faster onset than SC, safer than IV bolus.

whyChain.answer: Epinephrine counteracts every major feature of anaphylaxis: α1 (vasoconstriction, reduces angioedema), β1 (positive inotropy/chronotropy), β2 (bronchodilation, inhibits mast cell degranulation). No other single drug addresses all pathways.

questions.stem: She received one dose of epinephrine from EMS but remains hypotensive.
questions.question: What is the next step for epinephrine-refractory anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: She may need repeated epinephrine or IV administration.

options.text: Repeat IM epinephrine; if still refractory, start IV epinephrine infusion

questions.explanation: Repeat IM epinephrine every 5-15 minutes as needed (most patients respond to 1-2 doses). For refractory cases: IV epinephrine infusion (start 1-4 mcg/min, titrate to effect). Also give aggressive IV fluids (1-2L crystalloid bolus) for distributive shock.

questions.stem: Adjunctive medications are being considered.
questions.question: What is the role of antihistamines in anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: They are second-line, not first-line.

options.text: Adjunctive only - help with urticaria/pruritus but don't treat life-threatening features; give AFTER epinephrine

questions.explanation: Antihistamines (diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV/IM, +/- ranitidine/famotidine) are adjunctive. They relieve itching and urticaria but do NOT reverse hypotension, airway obstruction, or shock. Never delay epinephrine to give antihistamines. H1 + H2 blockers may have additive benefit.

questions.stem: She has significant bronchospasm.
questions.question: What bronchodilator can be added for anaphylaxis with severe bronchospasm?
questions.hint: Standard asthma treatment applies.

options.text: Inhaled albuterol (beta-2 agonist) for bronchospasm not responsive to epinephrine alone

questions.explanation: Albuterol nebulizer (2.5-5 mg) helps bronchospasm not fully relieved by epinephrine. Particularly useful in patients with asthma. Ipratropium can be added. Remember: albuterol doesn't treat hypotension or angioedema - epinephrine remains essential.

questions.stem: Corticosteroids are often given in anaphylaxis.
questions.question: What is the rationale for corticosteroids in anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Consider delayed effects.

options.text: May prevent biphasic reaction (delayed recurrence); not proven but commonly given
Proven to prevent all late reactions

questions.explanation: Corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 125 mg IV or prednisone 50 mg PO) are often given to potentially prevent biphasic reactions (recurrence 1-72 hours later). Evidence is weak, but they're low-risk. They have NO immediate effect - onset is hours. Never delay epinephrine for steroids.

questions.stem: You need to confirm the diagnosis of anaphylaxis.
questions.question: What clinical criteria define anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Multiple organ systems must be involved.

options.text: Acute onset with skin/mucosal involvement PLUS respiratory compromise OR hypotension; OR known allergen exposure with ≥2 systems involved

questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis is likely when ANY of: (1) Acute skin/mucosal involvement + respiratory compromise OR hypotension; (2) ≥2 of: skin, respiratory, cardiovascular, GI symptoms after likely allergen exposure; (3) Hypotension after known allergen. She meets multiple criteria: skin (urticaria), respiratory (wheeze, stridor), cardiovascular (hypotension).

questions.stem: She stabilizes but you're concerned about biphasic reaction.
questions.question: What is a biphasic anaphylactic reaction?
questions.hint: Symptoms can recur without re-exposure.

options.text: Recurrence of symptoms 1-72 hours after initial resolution, without re-exposure to allergen

questions.explanation: Biphasic reactions occur in 1-20% of anaphylaxis cases, typically 1-72 hours (most within 8-10 hours) after apparent resolution. The second phase can be as severe or worse than the initial reaction. Risk factors: severe initial reaction, delayed epinephrine, required multiple epinephrine doses.

whyChain.why: How long should she be observed?
whyChain.answer: Minimum 4-6 hours for mild-moderate reactions, longer (24 hours) for severe reactions, those requiring multiple epinephrine doses, or with respiratory compromise. She had severe features - consider prolonged observation.

questions.stem: You're preparing for discharge.
questions.question: What must be prescribed at discharge for all anaphylaxis patients?
questions.hint: Patients must be prepared for future reactions.

options.text: Epinephrine auto-injector (minimum 2), written anaphylaxis action plan, and allergist referral
No prescriptions needed if symptoms resolved

questions.explanation: Every anaphylaxis patient needs: (1) Epinephrine auto-injectors (prescribe 2 - reactions may require multiple doses, may need to replace used device); (2) Written action plan; (3) Education on use; (4) Medical alert identification; (5) Allergist referral for testing and management.

questions.stem: She asks about future shellfish avoidance.
questions.question: What dietary counseling should she receive?
questions.hint: Consider cross-reactivity patterns.

options.text: Avoid all crustacean shellfish; may need to avoid mollusks too; fish is usually safe unless separate allergy

questions.explanation: Crustaceans (shrimp, crab, lobster, crawfish) have high cross-reactivity (~75%) - avoid all. Mollusks (clam, oyster, scallop, squid) have some cross-reactivity - discuss with allergist. Fish (salmon, tuna) have different allergens (parvalbumin) - usually safe unless separate fish allergy confirmed.

questions.stem: She asks if she could have an allergic reaction to the epinephrine.
questions.question: Are there any true contraindications to epinephrine in anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Consider the risk-benefit ratio.

options.text: No absolute contraindications - anaphylaxis is life-threatening; benefits always outweigh risks
Elderly patients should not receive epinephrine

questions.explanation: There are NO absolute contraindications to epinephrine for anaphylaxis. The risk of untreated anaphylaxis (death) far exceeds any epinephrine risk. Use with caution in elderly, cardiovascular disease, but still give it. Pregnancy: give epinephrine - hypoxia/hypotension harm fetus more than epinephrine.

questions.stem: Despite treatment, her stridor worsens and SpO2 drops to 85%.
questions.question: What intervention is needed for progressive upper airway obstruction?
questions.hint: Airway management is the priority.

options.text: Early intubation before complete obstruction; may need surgical airway if unable to intubate

questions.explanation: Progressive stridor/angioedema requires early definitive airway management. Intubate before complete obstruction - angioedema makes intubation increasingly difficult. Have surgical airway (cricothyrotomy) ready if intubation fails. Nebulized epinephrine can temporize but doesn't replace definitive airway.

questions.stem: She is on metoprolol for another condition (hypothetically).
questions.question: Why are beta-blockers concerning in anaphylaxis?
questions.hint: Consider the mechanism of epinephrine.

options.text: Beta-blockers can blunt the response to epinephrine and worsen bronchospasm
Beta-blockers have no effect on anaphylaxis
Beta-blockers are the treatment for anaphylaxis

questions.explanation: Beta-blockers antagonize epinephrine's β-effects (bronchodilation, inotropy). Patients may be refractory to standard epinephrine doses. Treatment: higher/repeated epinephrine doses, glucagon 1-5 mg IV (bypasses β-receptor), aggressive fluids. ACE inhibitors may worsen angioedema.

questions.stem: Eight hours later, she develops recurrent symptoms.
questions.question: How should biphasic reaction be managed?
questions.hint: Treat like the initial reaction.

options.text: Same treatment as initial reaction - epinephrine, supportive care, monitoring
Antihistamines are sufficient for biphasic reaction

questions.explanation: Biphasic reactions can be as severe as or worse than the initial reaction. Treat the same way: epinephrine IM, supportive care, monitoring. This is why observation period is important and why patients are discharged with epinephrine auto-injectors.

questions.stem: Integrating all clinical information.
questions.question: Which statement BEST synthesizes this patient's presentation?
questions.hint: Connect trigger, mechanism, risk factors, and presentation.

options.text: IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to shrimp (cross-reactive with known crab allergy) with multi-system involvement including life-threatening airway/cardiovascular features, complicated by asthma history

questions.explanation: This synthesis captures: mechanism (IgE-mediated, cross-reactivity between crab and shrimp), severity (hypotension, stridor, bronchospasm = severe multi-system anaphylaxis), risk factors (asthma increases severity), and treatment implications (epinephrine essential, watch for refractory/biphasic).

questions.stem: Planning comprehensive discharge care.
questions.question: What is the comprehensive discharge plan?
questions.hint: Address immediate safety and long-term management.

options.text: Epinephrine auto-injectors ×2, action plan, strict shellfish avoidance, medical alert ID, allergist referral, consider observation for biphasic reaction
Antihistamines PRN and follow-up in 6 months

questions.explanation: Comprehensive plan: (1) Prescribe 2 epinephrine auto-injectors + train on use; (2) Written anaphylaxis emergency action plan; (3) Avoid all crustacean shellfish, discuss mollusk avoidance; (4) Medical alert bracelet/necklace; (5) Allergist referral for confirmatory testing; (6) Education on reading food labels, restaurant precautions.
